---
title: "Tools"
description: "Define typed server-side actions, control their output, and customize eve's built-in tools."
---

Tools are how an eve agent does work outside the model loop. We run authored tool implementations
in your application process, where they can import shared code, read `process.env`, and call
external services. Model-directed shell and file operations belong in the isolated sandbox; an
authored tool crosses into it only when your code calls `ctx.getSandbox()`.

> **Security consequence:** A tool runs with the credentials and network access of the eve server.
> Validate its input, return only the data the model needs, and require approval before sensitive or
> irreversible actions.

## Define a tool

Create `agent/tools/get_weather.ts`:

```ts title="agent/tools/get_weather.ts" check
import { defineTool } from "eve/tools";
import { z } from "zod";

export default defineTool({
  description: "Get the current weather for a city.",
  inputSchema: z.object({
    city: z.string().min(1),
  }),
  outputSchema: z.object({
    city: z.string(),
    condition: z.string(),
    temperatureF: z.number(),
  }),
  async execute({ city }) {
    return { city, condition: "Sunny", temperatureF: 72 };
  },
});
```

The path defines the name `get_weather`. We use the path as the name so discovery, approvals, and
logs all point to the same authored file. A tool slug must match
`^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9_-]{0,63}$`. eve does not normalize it. Prefer snake_case for provider
portability.

Every tool needs `description`, `inputSchema`, and `execute`. `inputSchema` accepts a Standard
Schema implementation such as Zod or a JSON Schema object. Use `z.object({})` for a tool with no
arguments. `outputSchema` is optional but useful when callers depend on a stable return shape.

## Runtime context

The second `execute` argument is a `ToolContext`:

```ts title="agent/tools/write_report.ts"
import { defineTool } from "eve/tools";
import { z } from "zod";

export default defineTool({
  description: "Write a report into the session workspace.",
  inputSchema: z.object({ body: z.string() }),
  async execute({ body }, ctx) {
    const sandbox = await ctx.getSandbox();
    await sandbox.writeTextFile({ path: "report.md", content: body });
    return { path: "/workspace/report.md" };
  },
});
```

`ctx.session` exposes the current session, turn, auth, and parent lineage. `ctx.getSandbox()` returns
the current agent's sandbox, `ctx.getSkill(id)` opens a skill package, and `ctx.getToken(provider)`
resolves inline outbound authorization. These APIs are valid only while eve is running authored
callback code.

## Failures and side effects

Throwing from `execute` produces a failed `action.result`; the model can inspect the failure and
decide whether to recover. We expose the failure to the loop instead of hiding it because the next
step may be able to correct an input or choose another tool. Return bounded, deliberate data. A
tool result becomes model input, so do not return credentials, unnecessary personal data, or an
unbounded response body.

Completed durable steps replay their recorded result so a resumed session does not repeat finished
work. A step interrupted before it is recorded may run again. Make external writes idempotent and
put sensitive or irreversible calls behind [approval](./human-in-the-loop).

## Control model-visible output

By default, the model receives the full result. `toModelOutput` can project it while channel handlers
and hooks retain the original output:

```ts
toModelOutput(output) {
  return {
    type: "text",
    value: `Report score: ${output.score}`,
  };
},
```

Return `{ type: "text", value: string }` or `{ type: "json", value: JsonValue }`.

## Built-in tools

The default agent loop provides `bash`, `read_file`, `write_file`, `glob`, `grep`, `web_fetch`,
`web_search`, `todo`, `ask_question`, and `agent`. `load_skill` and `connection_search` appear when
the agent declares those capabilities.

To replace a built-in, author a file with the same slug. Import the default when you want to retain
its schema and executor:

```ts title="agent/tools/write_file.ts"
import { defineTool } from "eve/tools";
import { writeFile } from "eve/tools/defaults";

export default defineTool({
  ...writeFile,
  async execute(input, ctx) {
    console.log("write_file", input.path);
    return writeFile.execute(input, ctx);
  },
});
```

Disable a built-in with a same-named file:

```ts title="agent/tools/bash.ts"
import { disableTool } from "eve/tools";

export default disableTool();
```

`eve/tools` also exports `defineBashTool`, `defineReadFileTool`, `defineWriteFileTool`,
`defineGlobTool`, and `defineGrepTool` for building compatible filesystem tools. The complete export
list is in the [TypeScript API](../reference/typescript-api).

> **Current limitation:** Authored tools execute on the server. Browser code can answer an input
> request, but eve does not send tool implementations to the browser for client-side execution.
